In wartime, as Madrid was [under siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Madrid "Siege of Madrid"), the government moved its capital to [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia,_Spain "Valencia, Spain") on 6 November 1936, and then to [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") on 31 October 1937. 2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_language "Catalan language") became official in Catalonia in 1932 as well as [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language") in the Basque Country in 1936. | |

(Spanish: *República Española*), commonly known as the **Second Spanish Republic** (Spanish: *Segunda República Española*), was the democratic government of [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") from 1931 to 1939. The Republic was proclaimed on 14 April 1931 after the deposition of King [Alfonso XIII](/wiki/Alfonso_XIII "Alfonso XIII"). It was dissolved on 1 April 1939 after surrendering in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") to the [Nationalists](/wiki/Nationalist_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Nationalist faction (Spanish Civil War)") led by General [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco").

The **Spanish Republic** (Spanish: *República Española*), commonly known as the **Second Spanish Republic** (Spanish: *Segunda República Española*), was the democratic government of [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") from 1931 to 1939. The Republic was proclaimed on 14 April 1931 after the deposition of King [Alfonso XIII](/wiki/Alfonso_XIII "Alfonso XIII"). It was dissolved on 1 April 1939 after surrendering in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") to the [Nationalists](/wiki/Nationalist_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Nationalist faction (Spanish Civil War)") led by General [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco").

After the proclamation of the Republic, [a provisional government](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_the_Second_Spanish_Republic "Provisional Government of the Second Spanish Republic") was established until December 1931, at which time the [1931 Constitution](/wiki/Spanish_Constitution_of_1931 "Spanish Constitution of 1931") was approved. Over the next two years of constitutional government, known as the [Reformist Biennium](/wiki/First_Biennium "First Biennium"), Prime Minister [Manuel Azaña](/wiki/Manuel_Aza%C3%B1a "Manuel Azaña") initiated numerous reforms. In 1932, [religious orders](/wiki/Religious_orders "Religious orders") were forbidden control of schools, while the government began a large-scale school-building project. A moderate agrarian reform was carried out. Home rule was granted to [Catalonia](/wiki/Autonomous_Region_of_Catalonia_(1931%E2%80%931939) "Autonomous Region of Catalonia (1931–1939)"), with a [parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Catalonia "Parliament of Catalonia") and a president of its own.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes195191-4)

Soon, Azaña lost parliamentary support and President [Alcalá-Zamora](/wiki/Niceto_Alcal%C3%A1-Zamora "Niceto Alcalá-Zamora") forced his resignation in September 1933. In the subsequent [1933 election](/wiki/1933_Spanish_general_election "1933 Spanish general election") the [Spanish Confederation of the Autonomous Right](/wiki/Spanish_Confederation_of_the_Autonomous_Right "Spanish Confederation of the Autonomous Right") (CEDA) won a [plurality](/wiki/Plurality_(voting) "Plurality (voting)"). However the President declined to invite its leader, [Gil Robles](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Gil-Robles_y_Qui%C3%B1ones "José María Gil-Robles y Quiñones"), to form a government, fearing CEDA's monarchist sympathies. Instead, he invited the [Radical Republican Party](/wiki/Radical_Republican_Party "Radical Republican Party")'s [Alejandro Lerroux](/wiki/Alejandro_Lerroux "Alejandro Lerroux") to do so. The period following the CEDA's victory, called "[black biennium](/wiki/Black_biennium "Black biennium")", was marked by escalating clashes between the left and the right and state-sanctioned repression against the left.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston1994-5) The CEDA exercised indirect influence over the government and in October 1934, was finally successful in forcing the acceptance of three ministries. A general strike was called by the [Unión General de Trabajadores](/wiki/Uni%C3%B3n_General_de_Trabajadores "Unión General de Trabajadores") (UGT) and the [Spanish Socialist Workers' Party](/wiki/Spanish_Socialist_Workers%27_Party "Spanish Socialist Workers' Party") (PSOE).[[4]](#cite_note-Payne,_Stanley_G_2008,_pp.84-6) The strike developed into a ['revolutionary' uprising](/wiki/Revolution_of_1934 "Revolution of 1934"), allegedly aiming to overthrow the Republican government, although [major action took place only in Asturias](/wiki/Asturian_Revolution_of_1934 "Asturian Revolution of 1934"),[[5]](#cite_note-7)[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston1994-5) where the rebels officially declared a proletarian revolution and abolished regular money.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne1993219-8) The rebellion was crushed by the [Spanish Navy](/wiki/Spanish_Navy "Spanish Navy") and the [Spanish Republican Army](/wiki/Spanish_Republican_Army "Spanish Republican Army"), the latter using mainly [Moorish colonial troops](/wiki/Regulares "Regulares") from [Spanish Morocco](/wiki/Spanish_Morocco "Spanish Morocco").[[7]](#cite_note-9)

In 1935, after a series of crises and corruption scandals, President [Alcalá-Zamora](/wiki/Niceto_Alcal%C3%A1-Zamora "Niceto Alcalá-Zamora"), who had always been hostile to the government, called for new elections, instead of inviting CEDA, the party with most seats in the parliament, to form a new government. The [Popular Front](/wiki/Popular_Front_(Spain) "Popular Front (Spain)") won the [1936 general election](/wiki/Spanish_general_election,_1936 "Spanish general election, 1936") with a narrow victory. The Right accelerated its preparations for a coup, which had been months in the planning.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston1983-10)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006-11)
Amidst the wave of political violence that broke out after the triumph of the Popular Front in the February 1936 elections, a group of [Guardia de Asalto](/wiki/Guardia_de_Asalto "Guardia de Asalto") and other leftist militiamen [mortally shot](/wiki/The_Assassination_of_Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo "The Assassination of José Calvo Sotelo") [José Calvo Sotelo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo "José Calvo Sotelo"), one of the leaders of the opposition, on 12 July 1936. This assassination convinced many military officers to back the planned coup. Three days later (17 July), the revolt began with an army uprising in [Spanish Morocco](/wiki/Spanish_Morocco "Spanish Morocco"), followed by military takeovers in many cities in Spain. Military rebels intended to seize power immediately, but they were met with serious resistance as most of the main cities remained loyal to the Republic. An estimated total of half a million people died in the war that followed.

During the Spanish Civil War, there were three Republican governments. The first was led by left-wing republican [José Giral](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Giral "José Giral") (from July to September 1936); a [revolution](/wiki/Spanish_Revolution_of_1936 "Spanish Revolution of 1936") inspired mostly by [libertarian socialist](/wiki/Libertarian_socialism "Libertarian socialism"), [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism") and [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism") principles broke out in its territory. The second government was led by the PSOE's [Francisco Largo Caballero](/wiki/Francisco_Largo_Caballero "Francisco Largo Caballero"). The UGT, along with the [National Confederation of Workers](/wiki/Confederaci%C3%B3n_Nacional_del_Trabajo "Confederación Nacional del Trabajo") (CNT), were the main forces behind the [social revolution](/wiki/Social_revolution "Social revolution"). The third government was led by socialist [Juan Negrín](/wiki/Juan_Negr%C3%ADn "Juan Negrín"), who led the Republic until the military coup of [Segismundo Casado](/wiki/Segismundo_Casado "Segismundo Casado"), which ended republican resistance and ultimately led to the victory of the Nationalists. The Republican government survived [in exile](/wiki/Spanish_Republican_government_in_exile "Spanish Republican government in exile") and retained an embassy in [Mexico City](/wiki/Mexico_City "Mexico City") until 1976. After the restoration of democracy in Spain, the government-in-exile formally dissolved the following year.[[10]](#cite_note-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Spanish_Republic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1931–1933, the Reformist Biennium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Spanish_Republic&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1931–1933, the Reformist Biennium")]

Main article: [First Biennium](/wiki/First_Biennium "First Biennium")

See also: [Background of the Spanish Civil War § Second Republic](/wiki/Background_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War#Second_Republic "Background of the Spanish Civil War")

On 28 January 1930, the military dictatorship of General [Miguel Primo de Rivera](/wiki/Miguel_Primo_de_Rivera "Miguel Primo de Rivera") (who had been in power [since September 1923](/wiki/1923_Spanish_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1923 Spanish coup d'état")) was overthrown.[[11]](#cite_note-13) This led various republican factions from a wide variety of backgrounds (including conservatives, socialists and Catalan nationalists) to join forces.[[12]](#cite_note-14) The [Pact of San Sebastián](/wiki/Pact_of_San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "Pact of San Sebastián") was the key to the transition from monarchy to republic. Republicans of all tendencies were committed to the Pact of San Sebastian in overthrowing the monarchy and establishing a republic. The restoration of the royal Bourbons was rejected by large sectors of the populace who vehemently opposed the King. The pact, signed by representatives of the main Republican forces, allowed a joint anti-monarchy political campaign.[[13]](#cite_note-15) The [12 April 1931 municipal elections](/wiki/Spanish_local_elections,_1931 "Spanish local elections, 1931") led to a landslide victory for republicans.[[14]](#cite_note-16) Two days later, the Second Republic was proclaimed, and King [Alfonso XIII](/wiki/Alfonso_XIII "Alfonso XIII") went into exile.[[15]](#cite_note-17) The king's departure led to a [provisional government](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_the_Second_Spanish_Republic "Provisional Government of the Second Spanish Republic") of the young republic under [Niceto Alcalá-Zamora](/wiki/Niceto_Alcal%C3%A1-Zamora "Niceto Alcalá-Zamora"). Catholic churches and establishments in cities like [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid") and [Sevilla](/wiki/Sevilla "Sevilla") were [set ablaze](/wiki/Burning_of_convents_in_Spain_(1931) "Burning of convents in Spain (1931)") on 11 May.[[16]](#cite_note-queabc-18)

#### 1931 Constitution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Spanish_Republic&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1931 Constitution")]

Main article: [Spanish Constitution of 1931](/wiki/Spanish_Constitution_of_1931 "Spanish Constitution of 1931")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Republique-allegorie.jpg/250px-Republique-allegorie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Republique-allegorie.jpg)

[Allegory](/wiki/Allegory_of_Hispania "Allegory of Hispania") of the Spanish Republic, displaying [republican](/wiki/Republicanism "Republicanism") symbolism such as the [Phrygian cap](/wiki/Phrygian_cap "Phrygian cap") and the motto *[Libertad, Igualdad, Fraternidad](/wiki/Liberty,_Equality,_Fraternity "Liberty, Equality, Fraternity")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Rep%C3%BAblica_Espa%C3%B1ola_1931%2C_Teodoro_Andreu.jpg/250px-Rep%C3%BAblica_Espa%C3%B1ola_1931%2C_Teodoro_Andreu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rep%C3%BAblica_Espa%C3%B1ola_1931,_Teodoro_Andreu.jpg)

*República Española* (1931) by Teodoro Andreu

In June 1931 a [Constituent Cortes](/wiki/Constituent_Cortes "Constituent Cortes") was elected to draft a new constitution, which came into force in December.[[17]](#cite_note-19)

The new constitution established [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech") and [freedom of association](/wiki/Freedom_of_association "Freedom of association"), extended [suffrage](/wiki/Suffrage "Suffrage") to women in 1933, allowed divorce, and stripped the Spanish nobility of any special legal status. It also ended Catholicism’s status as the state religion and imposed strict limits on the activities of religious orders, including prohibiting them from teaching; these anticlerical provisions were moderated slightly during the constitutional debates later that year. Its controversial articles 26 and 27 imposed stringent controls on Church property and barred religious orders from the ranks of educators.[[18]](#cite_note-20) Scholars have described the constitution as hostile to religion, with one scholar characterising it as one of the most hostile of the 20th century.[[19]](#cite_note-Arguing_Comparative_Politics-21) [José Ortega y Gasset](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ortega_y_Gasset "José Ortega y Gasset") stated, "the article in which the Constitution legislates the actions of the Church seems highly improper to me."[[20]](#cite_note-22) [Pope Pius XI](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XI "Pope Pius XI") condemned the Spanish government's deprivation of the [civil liberties](/wiki/Civil_liberties "Civil liberties") of Catholics in the [encyclical](/wiki/Encyclical "Encyclical") *[Dilectissima Nobis](/wiki/Dilectissima_Nobis "Dilectissima Nobis")*.[[21]](#cite_note-23)

The legislative branch was changed to a single chamber called the [Congress of Deputies](/wiki/Congress_of_Deputies_(Spain) "Congress of Deputies (Spain)"). The constitution established legal procedures for the [nationalisation](/wiki/Nationalisation "Nationalisation") of public services and land, banks, and railways. The constitution provided generally accorded civil liberties and representation.[[22]](#cite_note-payne_632-24)

The Republican Constitution also changed the country's national symbols. The *[Himno de Riego](/wiki/Himno_de_Riego "Himno de Riego")* was established as the national anthem, and the [Tricolor](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Second_Spanish_Republic "Flag of the Second Spanish Republic"), with three horizontal red-yellow-purple fields, became the new flag of Spain. Under the new Constitution, all of Spain's regions had the right to [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy"). [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia") (1932), the [Basque Country](/wiki/Basque_Country_(autonomous_community) "Basque Country (autonomous community)") (1936) and [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)") (although the Galician Statute of Autonomy could not come into effect due to the war) exercised this right, with [Aragon](/wiki/Aragon "Aragon"), [Andalusia](/wiki/Andalusia "Andalusia") and [Valencia](/wiki/Valencian_Community "Valencian Community"), engaged in negotiations with the government before the outbreak of the Civil War. The Constitution guaranteed a wide range of civil liberties, but it opposed key beliefs of the right wing, which was very rooted in rural areas, and desires of the hierarchy of the Roman Catholic Church, which was stripped of schools and public subsidies.

The 1931 Constitution was formally effective from 1931 until 1939. In the summer of 1936, after the outbreak of the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), it became largely irrelevant after the authority of the Republic was superseded in many places by revolutionary socialists and anarchists on one side, and Nationalists on the other.[[23]](#cite_note-payne_646-25)

#### Azaña government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Spanish_Republic&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Azaña government")]

Main articles: [Manuel Azaña § In the government](/wiki/Manuel_Aza%C3%B1a#In_the_government "Manuel Azaña"), and [Military reform of Manuel Azaña](/wiki/Military_reform_of_Manuel_Aza%C3%B1a "Military reform of Manuel Azaña")

With the new constitution approved in December 1931, once the constituent assembly had fulfilled its mandate of approving a new constitution, it should have arranged for regular parliamentary elections and adjourned. However, fearing the increasing popular opposition, the Radicals and Socialist majority postponed the regular elections, therefore prolonging their way in power for two more years. This way the republican government of [Manuel Azaña](/wiki/Manuel_Aza%C3%B1a "Manuel Azaña") initiated numerous reforms to what in their view would "modernize" the country.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes195191-4)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*]

Landowners were expropriated. Autonomy was granted to Catalonia, with a local parliament and a president of said parliament.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes195191-4)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] Catholic churches in major cities were again subject to arson in 1932, and a revolutionary strike action was seen in [Málaga](/wiki/M%C3%A1laga "Málaga") the same year.[[16]](#cite_note-queabc-18) A Catholic church in [Zaragoza](/wiki/Zaragoza "Zaragoza") was burnt down in 1933.

In 1933, all remaining religious congregations were obliged to pay taxes and banned from industry, trade and educational activities. This ban was forced with strict police severity and widespread mob violence.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHayes195192-26)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*]

The first military coup against the Republic was planned by groups of monarchist generals with the support of exiled monarchist ministers. The attempted coup which became known as *[Sanjurjada](/wiki/Sanjurjada "Sanjurjada")* ended in failure. Several hundreds of generals were dismissed, prominent monarchists were imprisoned or had to flee abroad, and the aristocracy was 'punished' with radicalization of the agrarian reform.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasanova201072–82-27) The coup contributed to the radicalization of the left.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

The extreme right did not abandon their plans of overthrowing the Republic, what was manifested in the foundation of the [Spanish Renovation](/wiki/Spanish_Renovation "Spanish Renovation") and a small fascist party *[Falange Española](/wiki/Falange_Espa%C3%B1ola "Falange Española")*, the rise of the militant [Carlist](/wiki/Carlism "Carlism") movement ([Traditionalist Communion](/wiki/Traditionalist_Communion "Traditionalist Communion"), *[Requeté](/wiki/Requet%C3%A9 "Requeté")* militias), and propaganda of legitimacy of a military uprising spread by such organizations as *[Acción Española](/wiki/Acci%C3%B3n_Espa%C3%B1ola "Acción Española")*. Fascism proper was yet a weak ideology, but such historians as [Julián Casanova](/wiki/Juli%C3%A1n_Casanova "Julián Casanova") describe the culture of the Spanish anti-Republican right as [proto-fascist](/wiki/Proto-fascism "Proto-fascism"), similar to Italian "pre-Fascism" and the German [*Völkisch* movement](/wiki/V%C3%B6lkisch_movement "Völkisch movement").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasanova201072–82-27)[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston200645–48-29) The anti-Republican right were supported within the Church and the army.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston200645–48-29) At the same time, the authority of the Republic was undermined by [an anarchist insurrection](/wiki/Anarchist_insurrection_of_January_1933 "Anarchist insurrection of January 1933") which culminated in the [Casas Viejas massacre](/wiki/Casas_Viejas_incident "Casas Viejas incident") of the anarchists and was followed by state repression.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECasanova201072–82-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Retrato_fotogr%C3%A1fico_del_%C3%B3leo_del_general_Jos%C3%A9_Sanjurjo_Sacanell.jpg/250px-Retrato_fotogr%C3%A1fico_del_%C3%B3leo_del_general_Jos%C3%A9_Sanjurjo_Sacanell.jpg)](/wiki/File:Retrato_fotogr%C3%A1fico_del_%C3%B3leo_del_general_Jos%C3%A9_Sanjurjo_Sacanell.jpg)

General [José Sanjurjo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Sanjurjo "José Sanjurjo"), the leader of the failed [1932 military coup](/wiki/Sanjurjada "Sanjurjada"). Having been granted amnesty in 1934 by the right-wing government of the Republic, he was one of the leaders of the 1936 coup.

### 1933–1935 period, the “Black Biennium” and miners' uprising

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Spanish_Republic&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1933–1935 period, the “Black Biennium” and miners' uprising")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Arrested_workers_during_the_Asturian_Revolution%2C_1934.jpg/250px-Arrested_workers_during_the_Asturian_Revolution%2C_1934.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arrested_workers_during_the_Asturian_Revolution,_1934.jpg)

Workers arrested by the Guardia Civil and Guardia de Asalto during the [Asturian miners' strike of 1934](/wiki/Asturian_miners%27_strike_of_1934 "Asturian miners' strike of 1934")

Main articles: [Background of the Spanish Civil War § The "black biennium"](/wiki/Background_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War#The_"black_biennium" "Background of the Spanish Civil War"), [Asturian miners' strike of 1934](/wiki/Asturian_miners%27_strike_of_1934 "Asturian miners' strike of 1934"), and [Revolution of 1934](/wiki/Revolution_of_1934 "Revolution of 1934")

Events in the period after November 1933, called the "[black biennium](/wiki/Black_biennium "Black biennium")", seemed to make a civil war more likely.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston200666–67-30) This period which followed the victory of the right-wing coalition in the elections, was marked by the state-sanctioned "preventive brutality" against workers' movements,[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston1994135-31) state-sanctioned repression against the left which included censorship, crackdowns and deportations of peasants,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston1994152-32) and violent clashes between the left and the right.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston200670-33) The period culminated in the failed socialist and anarchist uprising in Asturias which became known as the [Asturian Revolution of 1934](/wiki/Asturian_Revolution_of_1934 "Asturian Revolution of 1934").

The majority vote in the [1933 elections](/wiki/Spanish_general_election,_1933 "Spanish general election, 1933") was won by the [Spanish Confederation of the Autonomous Right](/wiki/Spanish_Confederation_of_the_Autonomous_Right "Spanish Confederation of the Autonomous Right") (CEDA). In face of CEDA's electoral victory, president [Alcalá-Zamora](/wiki/Niceto_Alcal%C3%A1-Zamora "Niceto Alcalá-Zamora") declined to invite its leader, Gil Robles, to form a government. Instead he invited the [Radical Republican Party](/wiki/Radical_Republican_Party "Radical Republican Party")'s [Alejandro Lerroux](/wiki/Alejandro_Lerroux "Alejandro Lerroux") to do so. Despite receiving the most votes, CEDA was denied cabinet positions for nearly a year. However, Although CEDA, the largest party in the parliament, did not formally join the cabinet, it exerted indirect influence because the government depended on its parliamentary support. CEDA reinforced this leverage by threatening to withdraw cooperation. Later, Gil Robles claimed that the governing Radical party was “carrying out CEDA's programme.”[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston1994128–153-34) The left viewed the CEDA not only as a threat to their progressive reforms, but to the Republic in general, and more to it, as a fascist party. The CEDA and its leader [Gil Robles](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_Gil-Robles_y_Qui%C3%B1ones "José María Gil-Robles y Quiñones") proclaimed their goals to dissolve workers' movements and parliamentary democracy, purge "Marxists, Freemasons, Separatists and Jews", and establish an authoritarian [corporate state](/wiki/Corporate_statism "Corporate statism"), praising [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") of [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy "Fascist Italy"), [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") of [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Engelbert Dollfuss](/wiki/Engelbert_Dollfuss "Engelbert Dollfuss") of the [Austrian *Ständestaat*](/wiki/Federal_State_of_Austria "Federal State of Austria").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston199466–71-35) Following the rise of militant revolutionary sentiments within the ranks of PSOE and UGT, [Francisco Largo Caballero](/wiki/Francisco_Largo_Caballero "Francisco Largo Caballero") adopted a revolutionary Marxist rhetoric which justified revolutionism as a way to combat rising fascism, uncharacteristic of European social democratic mainstream and the reformist traditions of the PSOE; the anarchists of the CNT followed a similar rhetoric.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston200610,_112,_119,_122-36)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston200691-37)

Socialists led by Largo Caballero decided to launch a 'revolution' if the CEDA entered the government, what, according to [Paul Preston](/wiki/Paul_Preston "Paul Preston"), Largo Caballero believed would never happen, hence there were no real preparations for revolutionary action in most parts of Spain. The decision to trigger an uprising if the CEDA entered the government although not publicly proclaimed was known in the government. After a year of intense pressure, CEDA was finally successful in forcing the acceptance of three ministries. The insurrection launched in response became known as the [Revolution of 1934](/wiki/Revolution_of_1934 "Revolution of 1934"). In most of Spain, it was suppressed with little resistance, and the Socialist leadership demonstrated its lack of preparation and will to fulfill their revolutionary threats.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston1994-5) A general strike was called by the UGT and the PSOE in the name of the *Alianza Obrera*. [Stanley G. Payne](/wiki/Stanley_G._Payne "Stanley G. Payne") believes that the issue was that the Left Republicans identified the Republic not with democracy or constitutional law but with a specific set of left-wing policies and politicians. Any deviation, even if democratic, was seen as treasonous.[[4]](#cite_note-Payne,_Stanley_G_2008,_pp.84-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Column_of_Guardias_Civiles_during_the_1934_Asturian_Revolution%2C_Bra%C3%B1osera.jpg/250px-Column_of_Guardias_Civiles_during_the_1934_Asturian_Revolution%2C_Bra%C3%B1osera.jpg)](/wiki/File:Column_of_Guardias_Civiles_during_the_1934_Asturian_Revolution,_Bra%C3%B1osera.jpg)

Column of Guardias Civiles during the 1934 Asturian Revolution, Brañosera

The inclusion of three CEDA ministers in the government that took office on 1 October 1934 led to a country wide revolt. A "[Catalan State](/wiki/Catalan_State_(1934) "Catalan State (1934)")" was proclaimed by Catalan nationalist leader [Lluis Companys](/wiki/Llu%C3%ADs_Companys "Lluís Companys"), but it lasted just ten hours. Despite an attempt at a general stoppage in [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), other strikes did not endure. This left [Asturian](/wiki/Asturias "Asturias") strikers to fight alone.[[36]](#cite_note-38) Miners in Asturias occupied the capital, [Oviedo](/wiki/Oviedo "Oviedo"), killing officials and clergymen. Fifty eight religious buildings including churches, convents and part of the university at Oviedo were burned and destroyed.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1977-39)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECueva1998355–369-40) The miners proceeded to occupy several other towns, most notably the large industrial centre of [La Felguera](/wiki/La_Felguera "La Felguera"), and set up town assemblies, or "revolutionary committees", to govern the towns that they controlled.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1977-39) Thirty thousand workers were mobilized for battle within ten days.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1977-39) In the occupied areas the rebels officially declared the proletarian revolution and abolished regular money.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne1993219-8) The revolutionary soviets set up by the miners attempted to impose order on the areas under their control, and the moderate socialist leadership of [Ramón González Peña](/wiki/Ram%C3%B3n_Gonz%C3%A1lez_Pe%C3%B1a "Ramón González Peña") and [Belarmino Tomás](/wiki/Belarmino_Tom%C3%A1s "Belarmino Tomás") took measures to restrain violence. However, a number of captured priests, businessmen and civil guards were summarily executed by the revolutionaries in [Mieres](/wiki/Mieres,_Asturias "Mieres, Asturias") and [Sama](/wiki/Sama,_Asturias "Sama, Asturias").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas1977-39) This rebellion lasted for two weeks until it was crushed by the army, led by General [Eduardo López Ochoa](/wiki/Eduardo_L%C3%B3pez_Ochoa "Eduardo López Ochoa"). This operation earned López Ochoa the nickname "Butcher of Asturias".[[39]](#cite_note-41) Another rebellion by the autonomous government of Catalonia, led by its president [Lluís Companys](/wiki/Llu%C3%ADs_Companys "Lluís Companys"), was also suppressed and was followed by mass arrests and trials.

The suspension of the land reforms that had been attempted by the previous government, and the failure of the Asturias miners' uprising, led to a more radical turn by the parties of the left, especially in the PSOE (Socialist Party), where the moderate [Indalecio Prieto](/wiki/Indalecio_Prieto "Indalecio Prieto") lost ground to [Francisco Largo Caballero](/wiki/Francisco_Largo_Caballero "Francisco Largo Caballero"), who advocated a socialist revolution. At the same time, the involvement of the Centrist government party in the [Straperlo](/wiki/Straperlo "Straperlo") and [Nombela](/wiki/Nombela_scandal "Nombela scandal") scandals deeply weakened it, further polarising political differences between right and left. These differences became evident in the 1936 elections.

### 1936 elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Spanish_Republic&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1936 elections")]

Main article: [1936 Spanish general election](/wiki/1936_Spanish_general_election "1936 Spanish general election")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Frente-popular.png/250px-Frente-popular.png)](/wiki/File:Frente-popular.png)

Image from the newspaper LA VOZ showing the leaders of the popular front elected in the Madrid constituency.

On 7 January 1936, [new elections](/wiki/Spanish_general_election,_1936 "Spanish general election, 1936") were called. Despite significant rivalries and disagreements, the socialists, Communists, and the Catalan-and-Madrid-based left-wing Republicans decided to work together under the name [Popular Front](/wiki/Popular_Front_(Spain) "Popular Front (Spain)"). The Popular Front won the election on 16 February with 263 MPs against 156 right-wing MPs, grouped within a coalition of the *National Front* with CEDA, [Carlists](/wiki/Carlism "Carlism"), and Monarchists. The moderate centre parties virtually disappeared; between the elections, Lerroux's group fell from the 104 representatives it had in 1934 to just 9.

American historian [Stanley G. Payne](/wiki/Stanley_G._Payne "Stanley G. Payne") thinks that there was major electoral fraud in the process, with widespread violation of the laws and the constitution.[[40]](#cite_note-42) In line with Payne's point of view, in 2017, two Spanish scholars, Manuel Álvarez Tardío and Roberto Villa García, published the result of a research where they concluded that the 1936 elections were rigged.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVilla_GarcíaÁlvarez_Tardío2017-43)[[42]](#cite_note-44) This view has been criticised by Eduardo Calleja and Francisco Pérez, who question the charges of electoral irregularity and argue that the Popular Front would still have won a slight electoral majority even if all of the charges were true.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

In the thirty-six hours following the election, sixteen people were killed and thirty-nine were seriously injured, while fifty churches and seventy right wing political centres were attacked or set ablaze.[[44]](#cite_note-Alvarez_Tardio_1936-46) The right had firmly believed, at all levels, that they would win. Almost immediately after the results were known, a group of monarchists asked Robles to lead a coup, but he refused. He did, however, ask prime minister [Manuel Portela Valladares](/wiki/Manuel_Portela_Valladares "Manuel Portela Valladares") to declare a state of war before the revolutionary masses rushed into the streets. Franco also approached Valladares to propose the declaration of martial law and calling out of the army. This was not a coup attempt, but more of a "police action" akin to [Asturias](/wiki/Asturian_miners%27_strike_of_1934 "Asturian miners' strike of 1934"), as Franco believed the post-election environment could become violent and was trying to quell the perceived leftist threat.[[45]](#cite_note-47)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006-11)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrenan1950300-48) Valladares resigned, even before a new government could be formed. However, the Popular Front, which had proved an effective election tool, did not translate into a Popular Front government.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrenan1950301-49) [Largo Caballero](/wiki/Largo_Caballero "Largo Caballero") and other elements of the political left were not prepared to work with the republicans, although they did agree to support many of the proposed reforms. [Manuel Azaña](/wiki/Manuel_Aza%C3%B1a "Manuel Azaña") was called upon to form a government before the electoral process had come to an end, and he would shortly replace Zamora as president, taking advantage of a constitutional loophole: the Constitution allowed the Cortes to remove the President from office after two early dissolutions, and while the first (1933) dissolution had been partially justified because of the fulfillment of the Constitutional mission of the first legislature, the second one had been a simple bid to trigger early elections.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrenan1950301-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Spanish_general_elections_of_1931%2C_1933_and_1936.jpg/960px-Spanish_general_elections_of_1931%2C_1933_and_1936.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spanish_general_elections_of_1931,_1933_and_1936.jpg)

Cortes composition, 1931-1936; size of rectangles proportionate to number of seats; parliamentary groups below 10 seats are ignored. A: Partido Agrario; AR: Acción Republicana; ASR: Agrupación al Servicio de la República; CEDA: Confederación Española de Derechas Autónomas; CT: Comunión Tradicionalista; DLR: Derecha Liberal Republicana; ERC: Esquerra Republicana de Catalunya; FRG: Federación Republicana Gallega; IC: Izquierda Republicana; LC: Lliga Regionalista de Catalunya; PCD: Partido de Centro Democrático; PCE: Partido Comunista de España; PRC: Partido Republicano Conservador; PRR: Partido Republicano Radical; PRRS: Partido Republicano Radical Socialista; PSOE: Partido Socialista Obrero Español; RE: Renovación Española; UR: Unión Republicana

The right reacted as if radical communists had taken control, despite the new cabinet's moderate composition; they were shocked by the revolutionary masses taking to the streets and the release of prisoners. Convinced that the left was no longer willing to follow the rule of law and that its vision of Spain was under threat, the right abandoned the parliamentary option and began to conspire as to how to best overthrow the republic, rather than taking control of it.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston1983-10)[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200619–39-50)

This helped the development of the fascist-inspired [Falange](/wiki/Falange_Espa%C3%B1ola "Falange Española") Española, a National party led by [José Antonio Primo de Rivera](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Antonio_Primo_de_Rivera "José Antonio Primo de Rivera"), the son of the former dictator, [Miguel Primo de Rivera](/wiki/Miguel_Primo_de_Rivera "Miguel Primo de Rivera"), although it only received 0.7 percent of the votes in the election. By July 1936, the Falange had a mere 40,000 members among millions of Spaniards.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In June 1936, [Miguel de Unamuno](/wiki/Miguel_de_Unamuno "Miguel de Unamuno"), disenchanted with the unfolding of the events, told a reporter who published his statement in *El Adelanto* that President [Manuel Azaña](/wiki/Manuel_Aza%C3%B1a "Manuel Azaña") should commit suicide as a patriotic act.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

### Assassinations of political leaders and beginning of the war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Spanish_Republic&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Assassinations of political leaders and beginning of the war")]

See also: [Assassination of José Calvo Sotelo](/wiki/Assassination_of_Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo "Assassination of José Calvo Sotelo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo.JPG/250px-Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo.JPG)

Calvo Sotelo dressed in the uniform of the Cuerpo de Abogados del Estado.

On 12 July 1936, Lieutenant [José Castillo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Castillo_(Spanish_Civil_War) "José Castillo (Spanish Civil War)"), an important member of the anti-fascist military organisation [Unión Militar Republicana Antifascista](/wiki/Uni%C3%B3n_Militar_Republicana_Antifascista "Unión Militar Republicana Antifascista") (UMRA), was shot by [Falangist](/wiki/Falange_Espa%C3%B1ola_de_las_JONS "Falange Española de las JONS") gunmen.

In response, a group of [Guardia de Asalto](/wiki/Guardia_de_Asalto "Guardia de Asalto") and other leftist militiamen led by [Civil Guard](/wiki/Civil_Guard_(Spain) "Civil Guard (Spain)") Fernando Condés, after getting the approval of the minister of interior to illegally arrest a list of members of parliament, went to right-wing opposition leader [José Calvo Sotelo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo "José Calvo Sotelo")'s house in the early hours of 13 July on a revenge mission. Sotelo was arrested and later [shot dead](/wiki/The_Assassination_of_Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo "The Assassination of José Calvo Sotelo") in a police truck. His body was dropped at the entrance of one of the city's cemeteries. According to all later investigations, the perpetrator of the murder was a socialist gunman, Luis Cuenca, who was known as the bodyguard of [PSOE](/wiki/Spanish_Socialist_Workers%27_Party "Spanish Socialist Workers' Party") leader [Indalecio Prieto](/wiki/Indalecio_Prieto "Indalecio Prieto"). Calvo Sotelo was one of the most prominent Spanish monarchists who, describing the government's actions as [Bolshevist](/wiki/Bolshevism "Bolshevism") and anarchist, had been exhorting the army to intervene, declaring that Spanish soldiers would save the country from communism if "there are no politicians capable of doing so".[[50]](#cite_note-52)

Prominent rightists blamed the government for [Calvo Sotelo's assassination](/wiki/The_Assassination_of_Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo "The Assassination of José Calvo Sotelo"). They claimed that the authorities did not properly investigate it and promoted those involved in the murder whilst censoring those who cried out about it and shutting down the headquarters of right-wing parties and arresting right-wing party members, often on "flimsy charges".[[51]](#cite_note-:0-53) The event is often considered the catalyst for the further political polarisation that ensued. The Falange and other right-wing individuals, including [Juan de la Cierva](/wiki/Juan_de_la_Cierva "Juan de la Cierva"), had already been conspiring to launch a military coup d'état against the government, to be led by senior army officers.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor200651-54)

When the antifascist Castillo and the anti-socialist Calvo Sotelo were buried on the same day in the same Madrid cemetery, fighting between the [Police Assault Guard](/wiki/Guardia_de_Asalto "Guardia de Asalto") and fascist militias broke out in the surrounding streets, resulting in four more deaths.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Jos%C3%A9_Sanjurjo_Sacanell.jpg/250px-Jos%C3%A9_Sanjurjo_Sacanell.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jos%C3%A9_Sanjurjo_Sacanell.jpg)

General José Sanjurjo Sacanell, Marquis of the Rif

The [killing of Calvo Sotelo](/wiki/Assassination_of_Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo "Assassination of José Calvo Sotelo") with police involvement aroused suspicions and strong reactions among the government's opponents on the right.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2001196–198-55) Although the nationalist generals were already planning an uprising, the event was a catalyst and a public justification for a coup.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPreston200699-56) [Stanley Payne](/wiki/Stanley_G._Payne "Stanley G. Payne") claims that before these events, the idea of rebellion by army officers against the government had weakened; Mola had estimated that only 12% of officers reliably supported the coup and at one point considered fleeing the country for fear he was already compromised, and had to be convinced to remain by his co-conspirators.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaynePalacios2018115-57) However, the [kidnapping and murder of Sotelo](/wiki/The_Assassination_of_Jos%C3%A9_Calvo_Sotelo "The Assassination of José Calvo Sotelo") transformed the "limping conspiracy" into a revolt that could trigger a civil war.[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeevor2006-11) The involvement of forces of public order and a lack of action against the attackers hurt public opinion of the government. No effective action was taken; Payne points to a possible veto by socialists within the government who shielded the killers who had been drawn from their ranks. The murder of a parliamentary leader by state police was unprecedented, and the belief that the state had ceased to be neutral and effective in its duties encouraged important sectors of the right to join the rebellion.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Within hours of learning of the murder and the reaction, [Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco"), who until then had not been involved in the conspiracies, changed his mind on rebellion and dispatched a message to [Mola](/wiki/Emilio_Mola "Emilio Mola") to display his firm commitment.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2012-60)

Three days later (17 July), the [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") began more or less as it had been planned, with an army uprising in [Spanish Morocco](/wiki/Spanish_Morocco "Spanish Morocco"), which then spread to several regions of the country.

Mola's plan for the new regime was envisioned as a "republican dictatorship", modelled after [Salazar's](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Oliveira_Salazar "António de Oliveira Salazar") Portugal and as a semi-pluralist authoritarian regime rather than a totalitarian fascist dictatorship. The initial government would be an all-military "Directory", which would create a "strong and disciplined state." General Sanjurjo would be the head of this new regime, due to being widely liked and respected within the military, though his position would be a largely symbolic due to his lack of political talent. The 1931 Constitution would be suspended, replaced by a new "constituent parliament" which would be chosen by a new politically purged electorate, who would vote on the issue of republic versus monarchy. Certain liberal elements would remain, such as separation of church and state as well as freedom of religion. Agrarian issues would be solved by regional commissioners on the basis of smallholdings, but collective cultivation would be permitted in some circumstances. Legislation prior to February 1936 would be respected. Violence would be required to destroy opposition to the coup, though it seems Mola did not envision the mass atrocities and repression that would ultimately manifest during the civil war.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne201267–68-61)[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaynePalacios2018113-62) Of particular importance to Mola was ensuring the revolt was at its core an Army affair, one that would not be subject to special interests and that the coup would make the armed forces the basis for the new state.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne201189–90-63) However, the separation of church and state was forgotten once the conflict assumed the dimension of a war of religion, and military authorities increasingly deferred to the Church and to the expression of Catholic sentiment.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne2012115–125-64) However, Mola's program was vague and only a rough sketch, and there were disagreements among coupists about their vision for Spain.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPayne201190-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)

Franco's move was intended to seize power immediately, but his army uprising met with serious resistance, and great swathes of Spain, including most of the main cities, remained loyal to the Republic of Spain. The leaders of the coup (Franco was not commander-in-chief yet) did not lose heart with the stalemate and apparent failure of the coup. Instead, they initiated a slow and determined war of attrition against the Republican government in Madrid.[[65]](#cite_note-iwm.org.uk-67)
As a result, an estimated total of half a million people would die in the war that followed; the number of casualties is actually disputed, as some have suggested that as many as a million people died. Over the years, historians kept lowering the death figures, and modern research concluded that 500,000 deaths were the correct figure.[[66]](#cite_note-68)

### Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Spanish_Republic&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Civil War")]

Main articles: [Background of the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Background_of_the_Spanish_Civil_War "Background of the Spanish Civil War") and [Spanish Republic at War](/wiki/Spanish_Republic_at_War "Spanish Republic at War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Ricardo_Miralles%2C_1938.jpg/120px-Ricardo_Miralles%2C_1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ricardo_Miralles,_1938.jpg)

Valencian Republican soldier

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Paracuellos_Lapidas_en_Fosa_II_Ruiz_Casaux.JPG/250px-Paracuellos_Lapidas_en_Fosa_II_Ruiz_Casaux.JPG)](/wiki/File:Paracuellos_Lapidas_en_Fosa_II_Ruiz_Casaux.JPG)

Victims of the [Paracuellos massacre](/wiki/Paracuellos_massacre "Paracuellos massacre") committed by the Republicans. The Republicans committed many acts of torture, murder, and war crimes throughout the war known as the [Red Terror (Spain)](/wiki/Red_Terror_(Spain) "Red Terror (Spain)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Spanish_Civil_War_-_Mass_grave_-_Est%C3%A9par%2C_Burgos.jpg/500px-Spanish_Civil_War_-_Mass_grave_-_Est%C3%A9par%2C_Burgos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spanish_Civil_War_-_Mass_grave_-_Est%C3%A9par,_Burgos.jpg)

A mass grave at the small town of [Estépar](/wiki/Est%C3%A9par "Estépar"), in [Burgos](/wiki/Province_of_Burgos "Province of Burgos"), northern Spain. It contains the remains of twenty-six republicans who were executed early in the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"), between August and September 1936, by fascists who belonged to [Franco's](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") Nationalists. It was excavated in July and August 2014.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/%D0%98%D1%81%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_11_%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B3%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B0_%D0%B2_%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B4_%D0%91%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%87%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5._1937-edit.jpg/500px-%D0%98%D1%81%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_11_%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B3%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B0_%D0%B2_%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B4_%D0%91%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%87%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5._1937-edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%98%D1%81%D0%BF%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_11_%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%B3%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%B0_%D0%B2_%D0%B1%D0%BE%D1%8E_%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B4_%D0%91%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8C%D1%87%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5._1937-edit.jpg)

[International Brigadiers](/wiki/International_Brigades "International Brigades") volunteered on the side of the Republic. The photo shows members of the [XI International Brigade](/wiki/XI_International_Brigade "XI International Brigade") on a [T-26 tank](/wiki/T-26 "T-26") during the [Battle of Belchite](/wiki/Battle_of_Belchite_(1937) "Battle of Belchite (1937)") (August–September 1937).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Paris_expo_1937.jpg/500px-Paris_expo_1937.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paris_expo_1937.jpg)

Spanish flag marking the place of exhibition of Picasso's painting *[Guernica](/wiki/Guernica_(Picasso) "Guernica (Picasso)")* in Paris during the World Expo in 1937 ([Agfacolor](/wiki/Agfacolor "Agfacolor")).

On 17 July 1936, General Franco led the [Spanish Army of Africa](/wiki/Spanish_Army_of_Africa "Spanish Army of Africa") from Morocco to attack the mainland, while another force from the north under General [Emilio Mola](/wiki/Emilio_Mola "Emilio Mola") moved south from Navarre. Military units were also mobilised elsewhere to take over government institutions. Before long the professional Army of Africa had much of the south and west under the control of the rebels and by October 1936, Time Magazine declared that "façade of the Spanish Republic was crumbling".[[68]](#cite_note-70) Bloody purges followed in each piece of captured "Nationalist" territory in order to consolidate Franco's future regime.[[65]](#cite_note-iwm.org.uk-67)
Although both sides received foreign military aid, the help that [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(1861%E2%80%931946) "Kingdom of Italy (1861–1946)"), [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") (as part of the [Italian military intervention in Spain](/wiki/Italian_military_intervention_in_Spain "Italian military intervention in Spain") and the [German involvement in the Spanish Civil War](/wiki/German_involvement_in_the_Spanish_Civil_War "German involvement in the Spanish Civil War")), and neighbouring Portugal gave the rebels was much greater and more effective than the assistance that the Republicans received from the USSR, Mexico, and volunteers of the [International Brigades](/wiki/International_Brigades "International Brigades"). While the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") wholeheartedly assisted General Franco's military campaign, the governments of France, Britain, and other European powers pursued a policy of non-interventionism, as exemplified by the actions of the [Non-Intervention Committee](/wiki/Non-Intervention_Committee "Non-Intervention Committee").[[69]](#cite_note-71) Imposed in the name of [neutrality](/wiki/Country_neutrality_(international_relations) "Country neutrality (international relations)"), the [international isolation](/wiki/International_isolation "International isolation") of the Spanish Republic ended up favouring the interests of the future [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers").[[70]](#cite_note-72)

Early in the war, the rebels won the [Siege of the Alcázar](/wiki/Siege_of_the_Alc%C3%A1zar "Siege of the Alcázar") at Toledo after a long siege. The Republicans managed to hold Madrid, despite a Nationalist assault in November 1936, and frustrated subsequent offensives against the capital at [Jarama](/wiki/Jarama "Jarama") and Guadalajara in 1937. Soon, though, the rebels began to erode their territory, starving Madrid and making inroads into the east. The north, including the Basque country, fell in late 1937, and the Aragon front collapsed shortly afterward. The [bombing of Guernica](/wiki/Bombing_of_Guernica "Bombing of Guernica") was probably the most infamous event of the war and inspired [Picasso's painting](/wiki/Guernica_(painting) "Guernica (painting)"). It was used as a testing ground for the German Luftwaffe's [Condor Legion](/wiki/Condor_Legion "Condor Legion"). The [Battle of the Ebro](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Ebro "Battle of the Ebro") in July–November 1938 was the final desperate attempt by the Republicans to turn the tide. When this failed and Barcelona fell to the rebels in early 1939, it was clear the war was over. The remaining Republican fronts collapsed, and Madrid fell in March 1939.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Spanish_Republic&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Economy")]

The Second Spanish Republic's economy was mostly agrarian, and many historians[*[attribution needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Attribution_needed "Wikipedia:Attribution needed")*] call Spain during this time a "backward nation". Major industries of the Second Spanish Republic were located in the Basque region (due to it having Europe's best high-grade non-phosphoric ore) and Catalonia. This greatly contributed to Spain's economic hardships, as their center of industry was located on the opposite side of the country from their resource reserves, resulting in immense transportation costs due to the mountainous Spanish terrain. Compounding economic woes was Spain's low export rate and heavily domestic manufacturing industry. High levels of poverty left many Spaniards open to extremist political parties in search of a solution.[[71]](#cite_note-73)
